concepts.name: Time Value of Money
concepts.theory_md:
## The core idea

A dollar today is worth more than a dollar later: invested at rate $i$, it grows.
Comparing cash flows means moving them to a common date with the **accumulation
factor** $(1+i)^t$ or the **discount factor** $v^t$, $v=\dfrac{1}{1+i}$.

Everything in FM is one principle: *value cash flows at a common time, then equate*
(the **equation of value**).

card.question: The time value of money principle says that:
card.answer: a dollar today is worth more than a dollar later because it can earn interest
distractors: money loses value only through inflation
a dollar later is worth more because of compounding
interest is irrelevant to comparing cash flows

concepts.name: Accumulation & Amount Functions
concepts.theory_md:
## a(t) and A(t)

The **accumulation function** $a(t)$ gives the value at time $t$ of $1$ invested at
time $0$, with $a(0)=1$. The **amount function** is $A(t)=A(0)\,a(t)$.

- Compound interest: $a(t)=(1+i)^t$.
- Simple interest: $a(t)=1+it$.
The effective rate in year $t$ is $i_t=\dfrac{a(t)-a(t-1)}{a(t-1)}$.

card.question: The accumulation function a(t) is defined as:
card.answer: the value at time t of an investment of 1 made at time 0
distractors: the interest earned in year t
the present value of 1 due at time t
the force of interest at time t

concepts.name: Nominal vs Effective Rates
concepts.theory_md:
## Converting rates

A **nominal** rate $i^{(m)}$ is compounded $m$ times a year; the **effective**
annual rate is
$$i=\left(1+\frac{i^{(m)}}{m}\right)^{m}-1.$$
Going the other way, $i^{(m)}=m\big[(1+i)^{1/m}-1\big]$. All rate forms ($i$,
$i^{(m)}$, $d$, $d^{(m)}$, $\delta$) describe the **same** growth — convert through
the effective annual rate or $v$.

card.question: How does a nominal annual rate i^(m) (compounded m times) relate to the effective annual rate i?

concepts.name: Discount Rate d vs Interest Rate i
concepts.theory_md:
## Interest vs discount

Interest is paid at period **end**, discount at the **start**:
$$d=\frac{i}{1+i}=iv=1-v,\qquad i=\frac{d}{1-d}.$$
Nominal discount: $d^{(m)}=m\big[1-(1+i)^{-1/m}\big]$. Also $v=1-d$ and
$(1-d)=(1+i)^{-1}$.

card.question: The annual effective discount rate d relates to i by:

concepts.name: Force of Interest δ
concepts.theory_md:
## Continuous compounding

The **force of interest** is the instantaneous rate
$\delta_t=\dfrac{a'(t)}{a(t)}$. For a constant force,
$$a(t)=e^{\delta t},\qquad \delta=\ln(1+i),\qquad 1+i=e^{\delta}.$$
For a **variable** force, accumulate by integrating:
$$a(t)=\exp\!\left(\int_0^t \delta_s\,ds\right).$$

card.question: For a constant force of interest δ, the annual effective rate satisfies:

concepts.name: Inflation & the Real Rate
concepts.theory_md:
## Real vs nominal

If the nominal effective rate is $i$ and inflation is $r$, the **real** rate
$i_{real}$ satisfies
$$1+i_{real}=\frac{1+i}{1+r},\qquad i_{real}=\frac{i-r}{1+r}\approx i-r.$$
The approximation $i-r$ is fine for small rates but the exact ratio is examinable.

card.question: Given nominal rate i and inflation r, the exact real rate is:

concepts.name: Equation of Value
concepts.theory_md:
## Set inflows = outflows at one date

An **equation of value** accumulates/discounts every cash flow to a common
**comparison date** and sets the values equal. The result is independent of the
date chosen. Most FM problems reduce to writing — and solving — one equation of
value.

card.question: An equation of value is obtained by:
card.answer: bringing all cash flows to one comparison date and equating inflows and outflows
distractors: summing all cash flows without discounting

concepts.name: Interest Rate Conversions & Compound Interest
concepts.theory_md:
## Compound interest & rate forms

$$FV=PV\,(1+i)^n,\qquad PV=FV\,v^n,\qquad v=\tfrac{1}{1+i}.$$
Simple interest accrues linearly, $A=P(1+it)$. Convert among
$i,\ d=\tfrac{i}{1+i},\ i^{(m)},\ d^{(m)},\ \delta=\ln(1+i)$ — all the same growth.

concepts.name: Variable Force of Interest
concepts.theory_md:
## Accumulating under δ(t)

With force $\delta(t)$, the factor from time 0 to $t$ is
$\exp\!\big(\int_0^t \delta_s\,ds\big)$. For $\delta_t=a+bt$,
$$\int_0^t (a+bs)\,ds = at+\tfrac{b}{2}t^2,\quad
\text{AV}=P\,e^{\,at+bt^2/2}.$$
Constant force is the special case $b=0$: $P\,e^{\delta t}$.

concepts.name: Annuity-Immediate vs Annuity-Due
concepts.theory_md:
## Timing of payments

- **Annuity-immediate**: payments at period **end**.
  $a_{\overline{n}|}=\dfrac{1-v^n}{i}$, $\ s_{\overline{n}|}=\dfrac{(1+i)^n-1}{i}$.
- **Annuity-due**: payments at period **start**.
  $\ddot a_{\overline{n}|}=a_{\overline{n}|}(1+i)$,
  $\ \ddot s_{\overline{n}|}=s_{\overline{n}|}(1+i)$.

Due = immediate $\times(1+i)$ (one extra period of growth).

card.question: In an annuity-due (versus annuity-immediate), payments are made:
card.answer: at the beginning of each period
distractors: at the end of each period

concepts.name: Level Annuities, Perpetuities & Loan Payments
concepts.theory_md:
## Level annuities

$$PV=\text{PMT}\cdot a_{\overline{n}|}=\text{PMT}\,\frac{1-v^n}{i},\qquad
FV=\text{PMT}\cdot s_{\overline{n}|}.$$
A **perpetuity-immediate** pays forever: $PV=\dfrac{\text{PMT}}{i}$. A level loan
payment inverts the PV: $\text{PMT}=\dfrac{L}{a_{\overline{n}|}}=\dfrac{Li}{1-v^n}$.

concepts.name: Annuities — Due, m-thly, Continuous, Non-level
concepts.theory_md:
## Beyond level annuity-immediate

- **Due**: $\times(1+i)$. **Perpetuity-due**: $\text{PMT}/d=\text{PMT}(1+i)/i$.
- **Deferred** $k$ years: $v^k\,a_{\overline{n}|}$.
- **Payable m-thly**: $PV=\text{PMT}\dfrac{1-v^n}{i^{(m)}}$.
- **Continuous**: $PV=\text{PMT}\dfrac{1-v^n}{\delta}$.
- **Increasing** $(Ia)_{\overline n|}=\dfrac{\ddot a_{\overline n|}-nv^n}{i}$;
  **decreasing** $(Da)_{\overline n|}=\dfrac{n-a_{\overline n|}}{i}$.
- **Geometric** (growth $g$): $\text{PMT}\dfrac{1-\left(\frac{1+g}{1+i}\right)^n}{i-g}$.
- **Solve for term**: $n=-\dfrac{\ln(1-Li/\text{PMT})}{\ln(1+i)}$.

concepts.name: Loan Amortization
concepts.theory_md:
## Splitting each payment

In an amortized loan, each level payment is part interest, part principal:
$$I_k=i\cdot B_{k-1}=\text{PMT}\,(1-v^{\,n-k+1}),\qquad
P_k=\text{PMT}\,v^{\,n-k+1}.$$
As $k$ grows, interest **falls** and principal **rises** (principal repayments grow
by the factor $(1+i)$ each period).

card.question: Over the life of an amortized level-payment loan, each successive payment contains:
card.answer: less interest and more principal
distractors: only interest until the last payment

concepts.name: Outstanding Balance: Prospective & Retrospective
concepts.theory_md:
## Two equivalent views

- **Prospective** (look forward): $B_k=\text{PMT}\cdot a_{\overline{n-k}|}$ — PV of
  the *remaining* payments.
- **Retrospective** (look back):
  $B_k=L(1+i)^k-\text{PMT}\cdot s_{\overline{k}|}$ — accumulated loan minus
  accumulated payments.

They always agree; pick whichever inputs you have.

concepts.name: Balloon & Drop Payments
concepts.theory_md:
## Uneven final payment

If level payments are **rounded**, the loan rarely clears exactly. A larger final
payment is a **balloon**; a smaller one is a **drop** payment. Compute by
accumulating the balance after the regular payments, then settling it with one
period's interest.

card.question: A larger-than-usual final loan payment that clears a rounded schedule is called a:
card.answer: balloon payment

concepts.name: Sinking-Fund Method
concepts.theory_md:
## Interest-only + side fund

Under the **sinking-fund** method the borrower pays the lender **interest only**
($Li$ each year) and separately deposits into a fund earning rate $j$ to accumulate
the principal $L$ by maturity:
$$\text{deposit}=\frac{L}{s_{\overline{n}|j}},\qquad
\text{total annual cost}=Li+\frac{L}{s_{\overline{n}|j}}.$$

card.question: Under the sinking-fund method of loan repayment, the borrower:
card.answer: pays interest to the lender and accumulates principal in a separate fund
distractors: pays only at maturity with no interest

concepts.name: Refinancing
concepts.theory_md:
## Restructuring a loan

To **refinance**, find the current outstanding balance (prospective is easiest),
then treat it as a **new loan** at the new rate/term to get the new payment. Any
fees are added to the balance being refinanced.

card.question: The first step in a refinancing calculation is to:
card.answer: find the current outstanding balance, then re-amortize it on the new terms

concepts.name: Bond Terminology
concepts.theory_md:
## Anatomy of a bond

- **Face/par** $F$: sets the coupon $Fr$ ($r$ = coupon rate per period).
- **Redemption** $C$: paid at maturity (often $C=F$).
- **Yield** $i$: the investor's required return per period.
- **Price** $P$: PV of coupons + redemption at yield $i$.
Coupon rate $r$ and yield $i$ are **different** rates — their relation sets
premium vs discount.

card.question: A bond's coupon amount each period equals:
card.answer: face value × coupon rate (F·r)

concepts.name: Bond Pricing, Book Value & Amortization
concepts.theory_md:
## Price and book value

$$P=Fr\,a_{\overline{n}|i}+C\,v^n.$$
**Book value** at time $t$ is the same formula on the remaining term:
$B_t=Fr\,a_{\overline{n-t}|}+C\,v^{n-t}$. Each period, the **premium amortized**
(write-down) is $\text{coupon}-i\,B_{t-1}$; for a discount bond this is the
**accumulation of discount** (a write-up). Coupons reinvested at $j$ accumulate to
$Fr\,s_{\overline{n}|j}+C$.

concepts.name: Premium vs Discount Bonds
concepts.theory_md:
## Which is it?

- $r>i$ ⇒ coupons exceed required yield ⇒ **premium** ($P>C$), book value
  amortizes **down** to $C$.
- $r<i$ ⇒ **discount** ($P<C$), book value accumulates **up** to $C$.
- $r=i$ ⇒ priced at par.

card.question: A bond sells at a premium (price above redemption) exactly when:
card.answer: the coupon rate exceeds the yield rate (r > i)
distractors: the yield rate exceeds the coupon rate

concepts.name: Callable Bonds
concepts.theory_md:
## Pricing for the worst call

A **callable** bond may be redeemed early by the issuer. To **guarantee** a minimum
yield, price it at the call date that gives the **lowest** price:
- Premium bond ($r>i$): worst case is the **earliest** call.
- Discount bond ($r<i$): worst case is the **latest** date.
Compute the price at each candidate date and take the minimum.

card.question: To guarantee a minimum yield on a callable bond, you price it at:
card.answer: the call date giving the lowest price
distractors: the call date giving the highest price
the average of all call dates

concepts.name: Yield Rates & IRR
concepts.theory_md:
## Internal rate of return

The **yield/IRR** of a cash-flow stream is the rate making
$\text{NPV}=\sum_t \text{CF}_t\,v^t=0$. No closed form in general — solve
numerically (calculator/bisection). For a single in- and out-flow it reduces to a
simple growth rate.

concepts.name: Dollar-Weighted vs Time-Weighted Return
concepts.theory_md:
## Two return measures

- **Dollar-weighted** (money-weighted) = IRR of the fund's actual cash flows;
  sensitive to **timing/size** of deposits. Simple-interest approx:
  $i\approx\dfrac{I}{A+\sum C_t(1-t)}$.
- **Time-weighted** = product of sub-period growth factors minus 1; removes the
  effect of cash-flow timing (measures the **manager's** performance).

card.question: Time-weighted return differs from dollar-weighted return because it:
card.answer: removes the effect of the timing and size of cash flows
distractors: weights early cash flows more heavily

concepts.name: Spot & Forward Rates / Yield Curve
concepts.theory_md:
## Term structure

A **spot rate** $s_t$ prices a zero maturing at $t$: discount each cash flow at its
own spot, $PV=\sum_t \text{CF}_t/(1+s_t)^t$. A **forward rate** is implied between
future dates:
$$1+f_{[1,2]}=\frac{(1+s_2)^2}{1+s_1}.$$

concepts.name: Duration & Convexity
concepts.theory_md:
## Interest-rate sensitivity

- **Macaulay duration** = PV-weighted average time:
  $\bar D=\dfrac{\sum t\,\text{PV}_t}{\sum \text{PV}_t}$.
- **Modified duration** $D_{mod}=\dfrac{\bar D}{1+i}$ measures price sensitivity:
  $\dfrac{dP}{P}\approx -D_{mod}\,di$.
- **Convexity** is the second-order term.
- **First-order estimate**: $\Delta P\approx -P\,D_{mod}\,\Delta i$.

concepts.name: Convexity
concepts.theory_md:
## Second-order correction

Duration alone is a straight-line estimate; **convexity** captures the curvature of
price vs yield. A better approximation:
$$\frac{\Delta P}{P}\approx -D_{mod}\,\Delta i+\tfrac12\,\text{Convexity}\,(\Delta i)^2.$$
Higher convexity ⇒ price falls less when rates rise and gains more when they fall.

card.question: Convexity improves a duration-based price estimate by accounting for:
card.answer: the curvature of the price–yield relationship (the second-order term)

concepts.name: Redington Immunization
concepts.theory_md:
## Protecting against small rate moves

A portfolio is **Redington immunized** at the current rate when:
1. $PV_{assets}=PV_{liabilities}$,
2. (modified) **durations match**,
3. **asset convexity $\ge$ liability convexity**.

This protects surplus against **small** interest-rate changes in either direction.

card.question: Redington immunization requires PV match, duration match, and:
card.answer: asset convexity ≥ liability convexity

concepts.name: Full Immunization
concepts.theory_md:
## Protecting against any move

**Full immunization** structures assets so surplus is protected against an
interest-rate change of **any size** (not just small ones), typically with one
asset cash flow **before** and one **after** each liability, matching PV and
duration. Stronger than Redington but needs a specific asset structure.

card.question: Full immunization (vs Redington) protects surplus against:
card.answer: interest-rate changes of any size, not just small ones

concepts.name: Cash-Flow Matching / Dedication
concepts.theory_md:
## Exact matching

**Cash-flow matching** (dedication) buys assets whose cash flows **exactly** equal
each liability as it falls due — no reinvestment or rate risk at all. It is the most
robust (and usually most expensive/constrained) strategy, requiring no rate
assumptions.

card.question: Cash-flow matching immunizes a liability stream by:
card.answer: holding assets whose cash flows exactly meet each liability when due
